videos.title: Avoid These TOURIST TRAPS in STOCKHOLM
videos.description:
Stockholm has a lot of amazing attractions, but it also has a fair amount of overpriced things to see and do. This video is all about tourist traps in the capital of Sweden.

For more videos every week, subscribe to the channel: https://www.youtube.com/threestarvagabond?sub_confirmation=1

Also, join the Three Star Vagabond Facebook group to discuss upcoming videos: https://www.facebook.com/groups/threestarvagabond

I've previously made a video about scams in Stockholm, but this time I'm focusing on things that just cost too much for the value they give you. None of these things are scams - they're all providing a good service of some kind, but you might still want to think twice about visiting them.

Aside from just listing things to avoid, I also give you alternatives to everything mentioned, so that you know what the cheaper alternatives are.

The things discussed in this video include:

- How to get to Stockholm City from Arlanda Airport. Taxi and Arlanda Express are just a bit too overpriced, since you can take Flygbussarna, Nettbus, Flixbus or public transport instead.
- Boat tours of the Stockholm canals are quite nice, but also quite pricey. Instead, you can for example rent a kayak or take a boat tour to the archipelago for a cheaper price.
-  The SkyView attraction on top of Avicii Arena (formerly The Globe Arena) takes you up 85 meters into the air so that you can get a lovely view of Stockholm. But then again...you also get a fantastic view for free from Skinnarviksberget or Monteliusvägen.
- Tipping is very common in some countries. Sweden is not one of those countries.
- Stockholm Old Town, Gamla stan, might be seen as a tourist trap. I'm discussing pros and cons about why it is or isn't a tourist trap, and also go into details about restaurants, gift shops and other details in the area.
- Alcohol is expensive in Sweden, but it doesn't have to be. I list some areas to avoid, and some places where you can find cheaper bars (Södermalm and Sveavägen).
- Night clubs in Stockholm sound like a tempting thing to try when visiting Sweden, but are they really all that good? I recommend some alternatives such as Syntax Error, Nalen, Debaser, or just regular old pubs or places like Laika at Hornstull.
- Finally, let's circle back to taxis. Aside from public transport, you also have options like Bolt, Uber and electric scooter. Or why not walk?

videos.title: Avoid These SCAMS When Traveling to Stockholm, Sweden
videos.description:
Stockholm is the 12th safest city in the world, but that doesn't mean that there aren't things to watch out for. In this video I tell you about scams to avoid when you're visiting the capital of Sweden!

You're not going to find a huge list of scams to watch out for, but that also means that you might be lulled into thinking that everything is safe in Stockholm. You always have to use common sense, keep an eye out for things that look out of place, and keep an eye on your belongings.

In this video I cover the following topics, among others:

- Taxi scams that unfortunately are completely legal in Sweden
- ATM credit card sniffing
- Pickpocketing and what to look out for in bars in general
- Illegal games on the street corners of Old Town (Gamla stan)
- Overpriced shops and restaurants
- The "picture taking" scam
- Scams within the housing market in Stockholm

I also discuss a bit of a sensitive topic, when it comes to the beggars that have become quite prevalent in Sweden lately.

But I also take some time to go through things that are sometimes listed as scams online, that absolutely should not be classified as scams. These include, for example, the Vasa musem, the Arlanda Express train and Gröna lund, a boardwalk amusement park.

Stay safe so you can make sure you enjoy the lovely city of Stockholm!

For more adventures every week, subscribe to the channel: https://www.youtube.com/threestarvagabond?sub_confirmation=1

Facebook: https://www.facebook.com/threestarvagabond/
Twitter: https://twitter.com/VagabondDeluxe
Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/threestarvagabond/

before you go to Stockholm looking for more travel tips make sure to hit subscribe and ring that notification Bell so firstly let's look at the best time to visit the best time to visit Stockholm is in the summer when the temperatures are the warmest and you can enjoy long sunny days however hotel prices are at their highest at this time too visiting at the beginning of June can give you slightly better hotel prices while September offers plenty of light but the temperature does begin to drop slightly so now let's look at getting there as the biggest city in Sweden Stockholm is a Transportation Hub for the region meaning that there are plenty of national and international connections to the city you could choose to fly to one of the two airports that aren't too far from the city with Stockholm Orlando Airport be in the biggest airport in the Country flights here are available from across Europe Asia and the Middle East from the airport you can take a bus or commuter train into the city center so now let's look at getting around Stockholm has an excellent public transport system that is made up of several bus lines commuter rails trams metros and ferries covering the majority of the city efficiently many choose to cycle around Stockholm as the city has plenty of bike lanes and paths to get around you could also choose to take a taxi but these are expensive so try to avoid if possible so now let's move on to the typical prices hostile dorm prices can be found for under 350 krona whilst Budget Hotels begin at two thousand four or five star hotels can be found for around 4 000 Kona breakfast cost around 50 lunch begins at 120 whilst dinner starts at around 200 krona you can get yourself a 24-hour travel card for around 130 or a seven day pass that costs 335 so Stockholm can be an expensive city to visit in Europe there are ways that you can save money on your trip firstly eating out in Stockholm can be expensive so save money in a few ways firstly enjoy lunch buffets and have a smaller dinner avoid sit-down meals visit the Asian restaurants or cook your own meals if you are renting an apartment or staying at hostels take advantage of the free walking tours we will leave a link in the description below for a few of our favorites but these are a great free experience that help you see the sites about costing you a thing you can also visit the museums some are free whilst offers offer free hours this includes the national museum of Sweden Museum of Natural History the Swedish History Museum and the Museum of Modern Arts you can also get yourself an unlimited Transportation card to save money on transport tickets and also get yourself a go City Stockholm card offering free entry to over 60 top attractions again we will leave the link in the description below so now let's look at the best things to do in Stockholm so this is a gorgeous City with plenty of things to see and do firstly head to the world-class museums including the Vasa Museum which includes a 17th century Salvage ship and the other Museum full of nostalgic memorabilia you can learn more about Stockholm at the Swedish History Museum and then head to the Lakeside saunas the Metro network is often referred to as the world's longest art gallery and is an attraction in itself and gorgeous sunsets can be watched from various points across the city you can take a cruise with get your guide we'll leave the link in the description below or enjoy a land and water tour to really explore Stockholm watch this video to find out how to save money on hotels when you travel then to find out some of the cheapest places to visit in Europe watch this video we hope you have an incredible time in Stockholm and stay creative [Music]

videos.title: How DANGEROUS Is Stockholm? Let's Visit a Stockholm No-Go Zone
videos.description:
People sometimes ask how safe Stockholm is to visit. Decades ago I would have said that it's absolutely safe, and that Sweden is one of the safest countries in the world. But things are changing these days...

For more videos every week, subscribe to the channel: https://www.youtube.com/threestarvagabond?sub_confirmation=1

Also, join the Three Star Vagabond Facebook group to discuss upcoming videos: https://www.facebook.com/groups/threestarvagabond

I decided to visit Rinkeby, a suburb of Stockholm that's infamous for having many low-income families and immigrants. It's been an area plagued with crime and unemployment - and it's one of the areas of Stockholm that foreign media sometimes calls a no-go zone.

But how dangerous is Rinkeby? And how dangerous is Stockholm - or Sweden - in general? In this video I dive into some statistics as well as anecdotal evidence, and I sprinkle it all with my personal thoughts on the matter.

Another reason I had to go to Rinkeby is that this day was Rinkebyfestivalen - a festival to celebrate the local community, and to build bridges across people and prejudices. Bands and artists were performing, food was served, and people were having a splendid time in the sun.

But how did my visit end? Spoiler: I didn't get shot when I was in Rinkeby. And I didn't get into any trouble at all. In my view there's no place in Sweden that's an actual no-go zone - but we do have areas with high crime rate, violence, and problems in general. But there's no place that deserves to term no-go zone as far as I'm concerned.

That said, Sweden has horrible problems with violent crime, gangs, assaults, and other incidents - especially compared to the rest of the Nordics. Sweden is still a safe country...but the question is for how long, if things continue along this line.

videos.transcript: Imagine a city where royal palaces, island hopping, and a hint of Nobel glamour all come together in one place. This is Stockholm. Some even call it the Venice of the North. And honestly, it makes sense. 14 islands. 57 bridges. Here, every island has its own vibe, its own rhythm. Medieval charm, contemporary lifestyle and nature just around the corner. Join us and explore the very best of Stockholm. Here we are, in Gamla Stan. The old town is the soul of the capital. Walking these cobbled streets is like stepping into a storybook. To start your day, make sure you've had a Swedish fika in a historic café. Because nothing kicks off a day in Stockholm like coffee and something fresh from the oven. Then head to Stortorget Square. Postcard perfect, but yeah, with a darker past. Ask your guide about it. You'll see. And if your coffee break already feels far away. Stop by Den Gyldene Freden. They've been serving up Swedish classics like meatballs since 1722. Oh, and don't miss taking a photo in Mårten Trotzigs gränd. It's the narrowest alley in town, ten cinnamon buns wide or 90 cm if you prefer the technical version. Now let's swap alleys for greenery and waterfront views. Welcome to Royal Djurgården, the world's first national city park. It's calm, it's beautiful, and it's full of some of Sweden's top cultural gems. Like this one, the legendary Vasa Museum. Inside? A 17th-century warship that sank on its maiden voyage. Not ideal. But the twist? We found it centuries later, almost perfectly preserved, and with some schnapps still inside. And today, it's one of Scandinavia's most visited museums. For another slice of living history, Skansen is the place. It's an open-air museum where you can stroll through old Swedish villages and even meet the locals. Yes, we mean the moose. And of course, there's ABBA The Museum. So warm up your vocals and shine on dancing Queen, karaoke style, with the kings and queens of pop. Speaking of royalty. Time to turn the spotlight on this one. The Royal Palace. 600 rooms, one of the largest in Europe. The King's official residence, also used for official receptions. Inside: royal apartments, grand halls and the Hall of State where the Silver Throne takes pride of place. Oh, and if you time it right, you can catch the daily changing of the guard. Uniforms, marching band, the full the royal show. Now, if you're after something straight out of a fairytale, hop on a boat to Drottningholm Palace. Think Versailles, but with a Swedish twist. First UNESCO World Heritage site in Sweden, royal residence, perfectly manicured gardens, and yes plenty of golden leaf inside too. In Stockholm, water is everywhere. And just a short boat ride from the palace, there's another masterpiece for your bucket list. Stockholm City Hall. You've heard of the Nobel Prize, right? Well, this is where the banquet happens. The big speeches, the dancing, the sparkle. So, even if you're not receiving the next world changing prize, you can still come here. Take the tour and see the Golden Hall for yourself. Over 18 million mosaic tiles. Yeah. Eighteen million. And if you're up for a bit of a climb, go up the tower in summer. The views? Worth every step. Think Stockholm's already giving you everything? Well, it also comes with 30,000 islands. Just outside the city. You can hop on a boat, rent a kayak or hike the newly opened Stockholm Archipelago Trail. Or just do what locals do: Grill a few sausages in the wild beauty of Sandhamn or Grinda. Or do it all. Because honestly, there's enough here to keep you coming back. In summer it's lazy beach days, sailing adventures. In winter? The whole thing turns into a snowy wonderland. The fact is, no matter when you'll visit. Falling in love is inevitable. Be ready. You've seen Gamla Stan, Stockholm's medieval soul. But have you made it to Södermalm yet? Just across the water and there's a whole new energy. A little more laid back, a lot more local. Start with Monteliusvägen. a favourite among Swedes for a reason. Skyline views and that soft light that makes everything feel cinematic. Not far from there, you'll find the city's slow fashion district a haven for vintage lovers and thoughtful design. Then head down into SoFo. Short for South of Folkungagatan. It's Stockholm's creative core: full of small labels, good coffee and that relaxed, creative energy Stockholm does so well. And speaking of creativity, Stockholm’s got serious cultural game. Take Fotografiska, for example. The photo exhibitions change regularly, and there's always something that sticks with you. For another cultural gem, take a boat to Skeppsholmen and grab an ice cream like a local. Whether it's snowing or 28 degrees in the shade, Swedes love ice cream. No exceptions. Then head to Moderna Museet. Here it’s modern and contemporary art, bold ideas, strong colours, pieces that stay with you. And just a short walk away, across a little bridge lined with houseboats, you'll find the Nationalmuseum. Different mood. Think Rembrandt, Carl Larsson, and beautifully restored galleries that feels like a calm, deep breath. Before heading home, don't forget to pick up a few souvenirs, the kind that lasts and the kind you might accidentally eat on the way home. Oops. For design lovers, Svenskt Tenn is a must. Timeless patterns. Elegant objects. Looking for something more affordable? Check out Skultuna, Design Torget, or one of many small boutiques across the city. Good taste doesn't have to break the bank. And for the foodies, Östermalms Saluhall is the place. This historic food hall has been serving Swedish delicacies since the 1880s. Gravad lax, Shrimp toast, pickled herring, you name it. Take your time, try a few bites and maybe pack a little something for later. Just saying, not everything makes it to the suitcase. And just like that, you've had a taste of Stockholm. A capital that's full of energy, ideas and breathing room. Here, you don't have to choose between culture or calm. You get both. And no matter when you come, summer, winter, in between. There's always something waiting for you. So what are you waiting for? Book the trip, pack your curiosity, and come feel it for yourself. Liked the tour? Give us a like, drop a comment, and subscribe for more Swedish adventures. And tell us, what are your must-dos in Stockholm? Welcome to Sweden. See you soon!
